text.title: User Control Implementation Patterns
text.summary: Control mechanisms (undo, cancel, exit, reset), undo vs. confirmation decision rule, and implementation patterns for modals, forms, and multi-step flows.

section.title: Control Mechanisms
section.p: Undo — reverse the last action; prefer this over confirmation for reversible operations. Redo — restore an undone action. Cancel — discard current changes and return to previous state. Back — return to previous screen in a flow. Close — exit current context (modal, panel, overlay). Reset — restore default settings.

rule.statement: Prefer undo over confirmation dialogs for reversible actions.
rule.rationale: Undo feels less disruptive, reduces friction for expert users, and does not interrupt the action flow; confirmation dialogs should be reserved for irreversible, high-stakes operations.

section.title: Undo vs. Confirmation Decision Rule
section.p: Use undo when: action is frequent, recovery is technically possible, impact is moderate (e.g., deleting a list item).
Use confirmation when: action is irreversible, impact is severe, action is rare (e.g., permanently deleting an account, canceling a subscription).

rule.statement: Use soft delete (move to trash/archive) by default for any content deletion; permanent delete requires explicit secondary confirmation.
rule.rationale: Soft delete gives users a recovery window and eliminates accidental permanent data loss without requiring a confirmation dialog on every deletion.

rule.statement: Every modal must have at least three exit paths: X button, Escape key, and clicking outside the modal.
rule.rationale: Users expect all three; implementing only one or two creates trapped-user scenarios for keyboard and mouse users respectively.

example.note: Gmail "Undo send" — action completes immediately, then a dismissible toast offers 5-30 second undo window. Removes the need for a "Are you sure?" dialog before sending.
Newsletter popup with no X button and no close option — user must subscribe or refresh page. Forced modal with no exit is a trapped-user antipattern.

rule.statement: Show step count and provide Back navigation on every step of a multi-step wizard except the first; provide a persistent Exit option throughout.
rule.rationale: Users who cannot see progress or navigate backward feel trapped and abandon; visible progress and exits reduce abandonment in long flows.

rule.statement: Do not use "Are you sure?" confirmations for non-destructive or routine actions.
rule.rationale: Confirmation fatigue causes users to click through without reading; reserve confirmations for genuinely destructive operations only.

text.reference: Playwright test for modal Escape key behavior
